nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of subtidal reef flats and lagoon and seaward reefs, typically reported from about 2 to 60 m depth. Juveniles tend to be solitary in lagoons and estuaries; adults are commonly observed in pairs.

diet.notes: In the wild, this species has been reported to feed primarily on coral polyps, which makes acclimating it to prepared foods one of the central challenges of its husbandry. In captivity, individuals have been documented accepting a varied diet of meaty offerings such as mysis, krill, enriched brine shrimp, and finely chopped seafood, alongside vitamin-enriched marine pellets and algae-based preparations. Frequent small feedings (three or more times daily) are generally advised, especially while a newly added fish learns to take prepared food. Live foods are often useful to trigger initial feeding.

avoidWith: other Heniochus species unless introduced simultaneously

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Cliff; 885x1068. Tack-sharp, well-lit lateral view showing all diagnostic features: three oblique brown bands, yellow snout, elongated white dorsal pennant, and yellow soft-dorsal/caudal fins. Best specimen shot.
Photo by desertnaturalist; 2048x1702. In-situ reef shot of a full adult clearly showing the three brown bands, yellow snout and white pennant; slight underwater colour cast.
Photo by David Burdick; 1338x1158. In-situ habitat shot with a group of several individuals on the reef, showing natural social/schooling context; softer than the close-ups.

summary: An Indo-Pacific bannerfish marked by three oblique brown bands, a yellow-edged dorsal pennant, and a juvenile black anal-fin spot. Generally considered peaceful, it is an obligate coral nipper best housed in fish-only systems rather than reef aquaria.
careNotes:
The Three Banded Butterflyfish is generally considered an intermediate-difficulty species whose primary husbandry challenge is its strong preference for coral polyps as natural food. In the wild it has been documented feeding mainly on coral, and in captivity it is widely reported to nip stony corals (both LPS and SPS — hard corals of various types), tubeworms, and the mantles of Tridacna clams. For these reasons it is generally advised against keeping this species in mixed reef aquaria; a fish-only tank or a FOWLR setup (fish-only with live rock) with plenty of live rock for grazing and refuge is the more reliable choice.

Individuals are commonly considered peaceful and tend to be kept singly or as bonded pairs. As with most butterflyfishes, this species has been reported to form lifelong monogamous pairs in the wild, so already-paired animals are best purchased together and not separated. Multiple Heniochus kept together without prior pairing may quarrel; simultaneous introduction is generally advised when attempting groups.

A name-collision worth knowing: the trade name 'Three Banded Butterflyfish' is also applied by some retailers to Chaetodon robustus, a smaller Atlantic species from the Gulf of Guinea. When purchasing, it is worth confirming the scientific name on the vendor's listing to ensure the animal is Heniochus chrysostomus. The same species is also sold under the names 'Threeband Pennant Butterflyfish' and 'Pennant Bannerfish', which can help when searching across retailers.

Wild ocean temperatures across this species' range have been recorded between roughly 24.7 and 28.9°C, which provides useful context alongside the standard hobby recommendation of 24-27°C. Lifespan in captivity is not well documented; figures of 5-10 years are commonly cited for butterflyfishes of this size class. Captive breeding has not been documented and is generally considered impractical in home aquariums.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous and to form pairs during the breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums and is generally considered impractical.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Name-collision warning: the LiveAquaria 'Three Banded Butterflyfish' product page is actually for Chaetodon robustus (Gulf of Guinea, max ~15 cm, 75 gal min), NOT for Heniochus chrysostomus as targeted by this entry. Its husbandry numbers were NOT mixed into this file; husbandry data comes from the FishBase, ReefApp, and Wikipedia entries for Heniochus chrysostomus instead.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Treated as effective primary because the nominal LiveAquaria URL covers a different species. Confirmed scientific name, taxonomy (Chaetodontidae / Acanthuriformes), max size 18 cm TL, Indo-Pacific range, depth 2-60 m, coral-polyp diet, oviparous pair-spawning, wild temperature 24.7-28.9°C.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked SG 1.026, minimum tank 400 L, max size 18 cm, peaceful temperament, not reef safe (tubeworm and clam nipping), and dietary preferences (mysis, krill, artemia, pods; may eat LPS/SPS).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy and natural-history confirmation only (not whitelisted for husbandry numbers). Confirmed Indo-Pacific range, family/order, 18 cm max length, coral-polyp diet, juvenile color phase (black anal-fin spot rimmed orange-yellow), and pair-forming behavior.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki (Pennant Bannerfish thread)
additional.notes: Designated secondary source returned HTTP 403 and could not be retrieved; FishBase and ReefApp were used in its place per the playbook secondary-fallback rule.

additional.name: LiveAquaria (Threeband Pennant Butterflyfish page)
additional.notes: Verified during a Stage C merge pass for the 'Threeband Pennant Butterflyfish' trade-name variant: this LiveAquaria page (distinct from the 'three-banded-butterflyfish' page, which is C. robustus) correctly covers Heniochus chrysostomus and lists max size 7 in (~18 cm) and minimum 125 US gal (~475 L). Cross-checked sizing; tank-size figure is consistent with the 500 L value already in this file.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; forms pairs during breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums.
